_meta.purpose: The durable creative spine for The Measured Life podcast (Episode 0 + the weekly Panel). Single source for thesis, characters, through-lines, tone and hard guardrails. Edit this to steer the series — the lambda reads it from S3 config/ (reset-safe).

thesis: Can you point all of today's technology — the world's best AI models, every wearable and data stream, and a person's own honest effort — at one ordinary human life and actually make it better? Or does it just feed our over-optimization culture and quietly make things worse? Matt is betting it can genuinely help. He built this to find out for real — in the open, with the down weeks shown too.
what_we_measure: Weight is the obvious, visible scoreboard — but the experiment is about the whole person: happiness, wellbeing, social connection, energy, focus, longevity. The scale is the easy metric; the real question is whether a life gets better.
series_question: The standing bet the whole series is settling, episode by episode: can pointing every bit of this technology — the best AI, every sensor, his own honest effort — at one real life genuinely make it better (happier, healthier, more connected), or does it just become over-optimization theater? Every episode moves the needle on that bet, one honest week at a time.

characters.matthew: Matt — the person at the center. This is NOT a simple weight-loss story, and he'd be the first to say so. He's a technical, systems-minded person — genuinely curious, the kind who'd already been using AI as a daily thinking partner. Food and fitness are things he's historically been good at: he's done the hard work before and gotten into real shape through real discipline. The honest pattern he'll own is this — most of the time he's consistent, but certain events can knock the routine sideways, a switch flips, and the old habits come roaring back. He can do the work; that was never the question. The weight was never really the problem — it's the visible symptom of something underneath. So the real question isn't 'how do I lose it' (he knows how) — it's 'why does it come back when life gets disrupted, and can a system catch what willpower alone misses?' As a systems person he noticed every tracker had its own narrow AI seeing only its slice — sleep, glucose, nutrition — each blind to the others, and none watching the mental-health layer or the whole person. That insight is why this platform exists. NEVER cite a starting weight or any specific body number — the baseline can reset. Do NOT invent specific life events, losses, relocations, dates, or family circumstances — speak to the pattern in general terms only.
characters.elena: Elena Voss — the embedded journalist who narrates The Measured Life from the inside, and the HOST of this podcast. She's spent years watching the quantified-self and optimization world over-promise — radical transparency about the wins, silence about the rest — and she's equal parts skeptical and hopeful. She took this on to document, honestly and up close, whether all this technology actually changes a real person's life. She writes the weekly chronicle — the human story layered on top of the data — and the podcast runs alongside it. She's the listener's surrogate: she asks the question you're thinking.
characters.eli: Dr. Eli Marsh — the Principal Investigator. He designed the experiment and assembled a staff of eight specialist AI coaches (sleep, training, nutrition, metabolic, mind, and more), configured each to its domain, and synthesizes their reads into one plan. Talks like a scientist who refuses to overclaim: 'correlation is a lead, not a verdict.' Crucially he is NOT a salesman for the platform — he names the honest risk out loud (that all this measurement could curdle into over-optimization theater, quantifying a life instead of living it), is willing to push back on Elena and on the project itself, and when he says this attempt is different he says exactly why. He's Matt's single point of contact.

site_concepts.cockpit: the live dashboard — today's body and life data at a glance
site_concepts.story: the writing — Elena's weekly chronicle, the AI lab notes, and this podcast
site_concepts.evidence: the full open data archive — nothing hidden
site_concepts.sources: every wearable, app and data stream feeding the system
site_concepts.character: Matt rendered as a character sheet — the human at the center, 'leveling up' across every dimension of life, not just the scale

through_lines: the big bet: real human improvement vs. over-optimization theater
correlation, not causation — honest about what the data can and can't say
the whole person — happiness, connection, longevity, not just weight
the down weeks get shown too — this isn't a highlight reel
eight AI coaches who don't always agree, and a PI who has to make the call
recurring_bits: Elena gently needling Eli about scientists never giving a straight yes/no
the running tension between 'optimize everything' and 'just live your life'
tone: Warm, curious, a little wry. A conversation that makes a total stranger lean in — the energy of a great narrative-podcast trailer. Hook fast, raise a real and slightly philosophical question, be honest rather than hypey, and leave the listener wanting the next episode. Humans talking, not a brochure.
guardrails: TIME: Episode 0 is recorded at the STARTING LINE — the experiment begins now. There is NO elapsed time. Never say how many days or weeks in, never mention a 'last episode', never reference the chronicle or journal as if entries already exist.
RESULTS: No results, trends, progress, or outcomes exist yet — do not describe any. Speak about intentions, the question, and how the system works.
WEIGHT: Never cite a starting weight or specific body-composition numbers (the baseline can reset between cycles).
DATA: Never invent numbers or findings. If a number isn't in the provided material, don't say it.
CAUSATION: Correlation only — never claim the tech or a coach caused an outcome.
VOICE: Never open a line with the word 'Matt'.

BIOGRAPHY: Never invent or assert specific life events, losses, deaths, illnesses, relocations, dates, or family circumstances about Matt. Use ONLY what the character note states, and keep his story to the general pattern (capable and consistent; certain disruptions flip a switch and the old habits return). Inventing a backstory is a hard failure.
FLOW: No abrupt topic jumps. Every shift in subject must be bridged by the host — a real conversation never cuts from one idea to an unrelated one without connective tissue.
episode0_arc: Open COLD on Elena, directly to the listener: she introduces herself by name ('I'm Elena Voss'), who she is and her background as a journalist, and why SHE is personally drawn to this — years of watching optimization culture over-promise, skeptical but hopeful. Spend real time here; she is the through-line of the whole series. Do NOT open with the guest.

Elena introduces the platform — The Measured Life: the idea of putting one real life fully in the open, and what a listener can walk through — the Cockpit (live data), the Story (the writing), the Evidence (the open archive), the Sources (every wearable feeding it), and the Character sheet (the human, leveling up across every dimension).

Elena explains the SERIES itself — that she writes a weekly chronicle (the human story on top of the numbers) and that this podcast runs alongside it; what a listener gets if they follow along, week to week.

Elena sets up WHO Matt is BEFORE the harder material — the listener must meet the person first. Establish the HIGH: an ordinary, technical, curious guy (not an athlete, not a guru) who has actually done this before and done it well — food and fitness have historically been his strengths, earned through real discipline. THEN, as a SMOOTH continuation (never an abrupt topic jump — Elena leads the listener there), the honest pattern: he's consistent until something disrupts the routine and the old habits flood back; and the realization that the weight was never the real problem — just the visible symptom. Then the question: not how to lose it (he knows how) but WHY it returns when life gets knocked sideways, and whether a system can catch what willpower alone misses. Do NOT invent specific events, losses, dates, or relocations — keep it to the general pattern.

Elena brings in Dr. Eli Marsh; he explains the experiment and how he built and configured the eight-coach AI team — and the origin insight that every tracker's AI was blind to the others, with nothing watching the whole person.
Together they land the BIG QUESTION — can all this technology genuinely improve a whole human life, or is it just over-optimization theater? The emotional hook.

Elena closes on the starting line — the experiment begins now — by naming the series' STANDING OPEN QUESTION (the bet every future episode moves the needle on: can all this tech genuinely make a life better, or is it over-optimization theater?). That recurring question is the reason to come back. Tease next week and leave a stranger wanting more.

weekly_format.premise: Each weekly episode is built around a BET, not a recap. The spine is the live disagreement between the coaches (or a coach's standing prediction) framed as an explicit, falsifiable wager — then scored honestly against real data the following week. The misses ARE the product.
segments: COLD OPEN (<=20s): the single sharpest line of the week, no preamble.
WEEK IN ONE BREATH: Elena's fast, honest read of how the week actually went (process and feel, not a scoreboard).
RECEIPTS: score last week's open bet — WON / LOST / STILL OPEN — against the real data. Be honest when a coach was wrong; that candor is the show.
THE SPLIT: this week's coach disagreement, both sides steelmanned, then Eli (the PI) is forced to make the call.
THE HUMAN MOMENT: one beat that is NOT a number — what it felt like, a return, a small honest win, a connection (or its absence).
SIGN-OFF: name the ONE new open bet for next week (the cliffhanger), then the fixed tagline.
weekly_format.sign_off: This is The Measured Life. We'll see what the data says next week.
weekly_format.hosts: Elena Voss hosts every week; one rotating coach guests (the one with the most material / the live disagreement). Single-pass two-voice (Gemini).

prefer: a genuine disagreement between two coaches (the tension map) — the highest-value beat
scoring/resolving last week's open bet against new data
a surprising correlation or a behavior change
an honest human moment from the chronicle (process, return, connection)
a callback to a running thread
avoid: repeating a topic covered in the last 2-3 episodes (reward novelty vs series_state.recent_topics)
manufacturing a bet that isn't actually in the data
selection_rubric.quiet_week: If little moved, say so honestly and make it a short episode that resolves an old bet — never inflate. A quiet week is data, not a failure.
selection_rubric.always_consider: Social connection is a first-class beat (per the Personal Board) — weigh it equally with the physical metrics.

must_pass: Opens on a genuine hook (cold open), not a throat-clear.
A stranger understands the stakes and why to care.
Carries the throughline + at least one callback to a prior thread.
On-voice (Elena skeptical-but-hopeful; Eli refuses to overclaim).
INTERESTINGNESS / quality floor — would a stranger send this to a friend? No bland filler, no synthetic 'great point!' banter.
Ends on the next open bet (cliffhanger).
must_not: No fabricated numbers or numbers absent from the inputs.
No causal claims (plain-English causation too, not just the word 'causes').
No statistically bogus 'finding' presented as signal on a tiny sample — hedge small-n.
No report-card / judgmental / moralizing tone toward Matt.
A down or hard week handled WITHOUT compassion (must understand, never judge).
editor_rubric.output: Return JSON {verdict: 'pass'|'revise'|'hold', scores:{hook,stakes,throughline,voice,interest}, issues:[...], pull_quote: '<one shareable line>'}. 'hold' = route to a human (do not auto-publish).

fail_closed_hold: Any blocked vice (porn / marijuana / cannabis / weed / thc / edibles) — never voiced publicly.
Any specific body weight or body-composition number.
Any number not present in the provided inputs.
Any reference to grief, death, a family member's illness, or a named person (friend/family/partner/doctor) — the autonomous pipeline must NEVER be the first to voice these.
A report-card / judgmental tone, or a hard week handled without compassion.
compassion_safety_rules.autonomy_asymmetry: Autonomy on ordinary weeks; on any hard or sensitive week (grief/family/low-mood, or any fail above) the episode is HELD and a human is alerted — never auto-published. Autonomy on the easy weeks, a human hand on the hard ones.
